title: Network effects: when A/B breaks
intro: The pizzeria tests a "bring a friend — you both get a discount" promo on a random half of its users. But friends are shared: the test group invites the control group, and the "clean" comparison falls apart. A regular A/B test rests on a quiet assumption: my landing in group B does not affect a user in group A. In social networks, marketplaces and delivery it collapses — and the naive group comparison gives a biased answer.

beats.text: The hidden assumption of A/B is called SUTVA: each user's outcome depends only on THEIR variant, not on what others got. User-level randomization is honest only when people don't interact. Often that is true — and then regular A/B works.
But picture a social network: half get the new feed (B), half don't (A).
beats.predict: A user in B starts posting more, and their friends in group A see it. What happens to the "cleanliness" of the comparison?
beats.reveal: The control gets contaminated by the test's effect: A also changes behavior because of B. The groups stop being independent, and the difference between them understates (or distorts) the true effect. This is interference between units — the classic failure of A/B in networks.

beats.text: Where it bites hardest: social networks (friends in different groups), marketplaces and delivery (shared sellers/couriers — if B takes more couriers, A is left without them), messengers (two-way communication), any two-sided market with a shared pool of resources.

The cure is to change the unit of randomization so the interaction stays INSIDE a group. Press "cluster randomization" on the chart: almost no red (contaminated) A↔B links remain. That is the trick — split not people but whole communities/cities. Geo experiments: an entire region in one arm. Switchback: the whole market is switched between A and B over time. That way interference doesn't "leak" between arms.

And how do you tell that SUTVA is actually violated, rather than just fear it? The most practical way — look at the bottom chart: estimate the effect two ways and compare. Under interference, the naive estimate (user split) and the clean one (cluster/geo) diverge noticeably: with a user split the effect leaks into the control and the estimate is understated. Toggle the design on the chart — in user-split mode the gap is large (a red flag), with cluster randomization it nearly disappears. A large gap between designs is the indicator of a SUTVA violation. In practice, a budget geo or cluster check is run alongside the main test for exactly this.

The core skill: before launch, ask "can my users influence each other?". If yes — a regular user-level A/B will give a biased result, and you need a network/cluster design. This is one of the most common places where an otherwise competent experiment fails silently.

definitions.text: the assumption that a user's outcome depends only on their own variant, not on others' variants. The foundation of regular A/B.
definitions.simple: the assumption: my test variant doesn't affect your behavior.

definitions.text: a SUTVA violation: groups influence each other (social networks, shared resources), and the comparison is biased.
definitions.simple: the groups "leak" into each other — the effect gets mixed.

definitions.text: split not people but groups (communities, cities), so the interaction stays inside an arm.
definitions.simple: split whole cities or groups, not people.

definitions.text: switch the whole market between A and B over time — a design for markets with a shared pool of resources.
definitions.simple: switch the whole product back and forth over time.

decision: If users influence each other, a regular A/B will give a biased answer. The design decision — clusters or switchback — is made before launch.
practice: Before an A/B on a platform where users interact, think about the unit of randomization right away: people, clusters, geo or time. Getting this wrong means a pretty but wrong result that falls apart at rollout.
The telltale of interference is shared resources or communication between users. A marketplace with a shared courier pool almost always calls for a geo or switchback experiment, not a user split.
realLife: Social networks test viral mechanics by clusters (by community) — otherwise the effect "leaks" through friendship ties into the control.
Delivery and ride-hailing services use switchback and geo experiments: if the test takes couriers away from the control, a regular user split will show a false result.
assumptions: Cluster and geo designs are more honest under interference, but costlier: the effective sample size is the number of clusters, not people, so you need many clusters for power.
Switchback assumes the effect shows up quickly and there is no strong carryover between periods; with long delayed effects it is biased too.
